Planning Your First Backpacking Trip

By Sarah Chen | March 12, 2026

The first time I went backpacking, I over-packed by about 15 pounds. My pack weighed 52 lbs for a three-day trip in the Rockies. By mile six on day one, I was sitting on a rock questioning every decision I had ever made.

Pack weight is the most important variable for a comfortable trip. There is a simple rule that helps: your pack should weigh no more than 20% of your body weight. For most adults, that's somewhere between 25 and 35 pounds including water. Once you start cutting weight ruthlessly — ditching the camp chair, going with a tarp instead of a tent — it becomes almost addictive.

The three-season sleeping bag is the most confusing purchase because the temperature ratings are optimistic. A bag rated to 30°F will keep most people alive at 30°F, not comfortable. Most experienced backpackers recommend buying a bag rated 10-15 degrees colder than the lowest temperature you expect to encounter.

Sarah Chen is a gear editor with 12 years of experience covering outdoor equipment. She has completed the Colorado Trail twice and tested gear in conditions from desert heat to alpine winter. She lives in Fort Collins, Colorado with her two dogs.
